coredata.dc:description: This review paper explores the burgeoning field of green synthesis of carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs) and their profound implications in addressing antibacterial resistance. Quantum dots (QDs), including CQDs, are nano-sized fluorescent particles with diverse applications in areas like medical technology, sensing, and imaging. CQDs, which are carbon-based and exhibit remarkable biocompatibility, low toxicity, and tunable emission properties, have recently garnered substantial attention. This paper delves into the “green synthesis” of CQDs, emphasizing eco-friendly, sustainable methods using plant extracts and microbial agents. The exploration of CQDs' antibacterial properties is a central focus, revealing their remarkable antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. These findings suggest a wide spectrum of antibacterial potential, offering innovative approaches to combating infections and enhancing public health. Mechanisms underlying CQDs' antibacterial efficacy, including the generation of reactive oxygen species, direct interaction with bacterial membranes, and disruption of biofilms, are discussed. To optimize their application, further research is required. Moreover, the review comprehensively categorizes quantum dots into semiconductor, metallic, and carbon-based types, and elucidates the properties and structure of CQDs. The classification of CQDs into distinct groups based on structural features is examined, shedding light on this multifaceted class of nanomaterials. The synthesis and characterization of CQDs are also detailed, highlighting the importance of precision in tailoring their properties for various applications. This review paper provides a comprehensive understanding of CQDs and their potential for combatting antibacterial resistance, offering valuable insights into this cutting-edge field.

row.entry: Synthesis Method
Principle
Advantages
Applications
References
Hydrothermal
High-temperature water-based synthesis method
Simplicity, scalability, and versatility. Controlled size and surface functionalization.
Bioimaging, sensing, drug delivery, photocatalysis.
Microwave-Assisted
Utilizes microwave energy to drive synthesis reactions
Rapid and controlled synthesis. Green and energy-efficient. Tunable properties.
Optoelectronics, sensors, drug delivery, and nanocomposites.
Ultrasonication
Uses high-frequency sound waves for synthesis
Eco-friendly and energy-efficient. Precise control of properties. Good water dispersibility.
Biomedicine, sensing, photocatalysis, and environmental remediation.
Biomass-Derived Precursors
Utilizes sustainable carbon sources from biomass
Environmentally friendly and cost-effective. Tailorable properties. Low toxicity.
Biomedicine, sensors, environmental applications, and sustainable material production.
Microorganism-Mediated
Employs microorganisms as biocatalysts for synthesis
Green and sustainable. Mild reaction conditions. Tunable properties.
Biomedical imaging, drug delivery, and biotechnology.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This review paper explores the burgeoning field of green synthesis of carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs) and their profound implications in addressing antibacterial resistance. Quantum dots (QDs), including CQDs, are nano-sized fluorescent particles with diverse applications in areas like medical technology, sensing, and imaging. CQDs, which are carbon-based and exhibit remarkable biocompatibility, low toxicity, and tunable emission properties, have recently garnered substantial attention. This paper delves into the “green synthesis” of CQDs, emphasizing eco-friendly, sustainable methods using plant extracts and microbial agents. The exploration of CQDs' antibacterial properties is a central focus, revealing their remarkable antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. These findings suggest a wide spectrum of antibacterial potential, offering innovative approaches to combating infections and enhancing public health. Mechanisms underlying CQDs' antibacterial efficacy, including the generation of reactive oxygen species, direct interaction with bacterial membranes, and disruption of biofilms, are discussed. To optimize their application, further research is required. Moreover, the review comprehensively categorizes quantum dots into semiconductor, metallic, and carbon-based types, and elucidates the properties and structure of CQDs. The classification of CQDs into distinct groups based on structural features is examined, shedding light on this multifaceted class of nanomaterials. The synthesis and characterization of CQDs are also detailed, highlighting the importance of precision in tailoring their properties for various applications. This review paper provides a comprehensive understanding of CQDs and their potential for combatting antibacterial resistance, offering valuable insights into this cutting-edge field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) represent a diverse group of small, intensely fluorescent nanoparticles and have emerged as a promising material class with a wide range of applications in sensing, medical technology, and imaging [1,2]. Each QD is akin to a minute concentration of matter condensed into a singular point. Within this minuscule entity, electrically charged particles are confined, possessing well-defined energy levels dictated by the principles of quantum theory, resembling the behavior of individual atoms [3]. Typically, QDs exhibit diameters spanning from 1 nm to several tens of nanometers, grown from or etched into semiconductor materials. As a result, they generally consist of merely a few dozen atoms and comprise anywhere from a hundred to a few thousand atoms [4]. Constructed from semiconductors like silicon, they possess crystalline properties but behave akin to individual atoms, earning them the moniker “artificial atoms,” denoting their minute size where charge and energy levels are quantized. These nanoparticles, known for their intense luminescence and uniform nanocrystal structure, have captivated considerable interest due to their applicability as labels in bioanalytical procedures [5]. Generally, nanostructures have many applications in various fields such as sensors, photocatalyst, antibacterial, drug delivery and other uses [6–8]. Quantum dots also have many applications in the mentioned fields. Recently, the extraordinary attributes of QDs have gained significant traction in the biomedical domain, facilitating real-time tissue imaging (bioimaging), diagnostics, single-molecule probes, and antibacterial activity, drug delivery, among a myriad of other applications. QDs offer advantages over traditional materials, such as polymer or silica spheres, in antibacterial systems due to their eco-friendliness and lower toxicity [9]. Noteworthy benefits of semiconductor-based QDs encompass their adjustable bandgap, high absorption coefficients, the ability to generate multiple excitons with single-photon absorption, facile formation into various structures (e.g., sheets or 3D arrays), cost-effectiveness, and mass producibility. Additionally, QDs are highly suitable for many applications owing to their elevated surface-to-volume ratio, heightened reactivity, small dimensions, and electronic properties influenced by geometry [10].

Quantum dots (QDs) can be broadly classified into three main types based on their composition: Semiconductor Quantum Dots [11], metallic Quantum Dots [12], and carbon-Based Quantum Dots. In addition to these main categories, there are hybrid and composite quantum dots, where multiple materials or types of quantum dots are combined to harness the advantages of each component [13]. These hybrid structures can exhibit enhanced properties or functionalities compared to individual quantum dots [14]. Among them, carbon-based quantum dots are composed of carbon atoms and are gaining increasing attention in recent years. They can be derived from carbon-rich sources like carbon nanotubes, graphene, or organic precursors. Carbon quantum dots exhibit remarkable properties, including excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and tunable emission properties. They have applications in bioimaging, antibacterial, drug delivery, energy conversion, and sensing [15].

Green synthesis of quantum dots involves producing these nanocrystals using environmentally friendly and sustainable methods. The term “green” signifies the use of non-toxic, eco-friendly, and renewable materials or solvents, aiming to minimize environmental impact during the synthesis process [16]. Green synthesis methods often utilize plant extracts, microbes, or other bio-based materials as reducing and stabilizing agents to produce quantum dots [17]. These green synthesis methods offer advantages in terms of sustainability, reduced environmental impact, and scalability, making them desirable for producing carbon quantum dots for various applications in a more eco-friendly manner [3,18].

Different sciences have made great progress in different fields [19–22]. Among these sciences, we can mention chemistry, physics, mathematics, biology, and others [23–25]. Nanoscience is no exception to this rule, and the production of various nanoparticles has led to progress in various fields, including antibacterial, drug delivery, pharmacy, photocatalyst, sensors [26–28], etc. CQDs have become a focal point of research and garnered substantial interest due to their potential antibacterial properties. In recent years, there has been a surge of investigations into the antibacterial capabilities of CQDs, driving the understanding of their mechanisms and applications [29,30]. Ongoing research in this realm is dynamic and ever-evolving, aiming to unlock the full potential of CQDs as effective antibacterial agents. Studies conducted thus far provide compelling evidence indicating that CQDs possess remarkable antibacterial activity against a diverse spectrum of bacteria, encompassing both Gram-positive and Gram-negative strains [31]. These findings suggest a broad-spectrum antibacterial potential, hinting at the possibility of employing CQDs in diverse antibacterial applications for combating infections and enhancing public health [32]. The multifaceted nature of CQDs as antibacterial agents is a burgeoning area of research, with promising implications for future biomedical and therapeutic advancements [33]. Further exploration and in-depth investigations are anticipated to illuminate the precise mechanisms and optimize the antibacterial efficacy of CQDs, paving the way for innovative antibacterial strategies and applications [34].

The review provides a comprehensive overview of quantum dots, encompassing their introduction and the innovative approach of green synthesis. Additionally, the review delves into the intricate structure of quantum dots, shedding light on their composition, size, shape, and potential surface modifications, crucial for tailoring their properties to specific applications. Of notable importance, the review delves into the fascinating realm of antibacterial resistance exhibited by quantum dots. This investigation offers insights into their potential as effective antibacterial agents, demonstrating the growing significance of quantum dots in addressing the global challenge of bacterial resistance. By examining these vital aspects, the review provides a valuable and comprehensive understanding of quantum dots and their promising applications in antibacterial research.

QDs can be broadly classified into three main types based on their composition:
I.
Semiconductor Quantum Dots
II.
Metallic Quantum Dots
III.
Carbon-Based Quantum Dots

Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) are nanoscale semiconductor crystals that have emerged as a focal point of research and technological advancement. Comprising inorganic semiconductor materials like cadmium selenide (CdSe), cadmium telluride (CdTe), and others, these structures exhibit unique quantum confinement effects due to their small size, typically less than 10 nm [35]. This quantum confinement leads to discrete energy levels, influencing their optical and electronic properties. The size and composition of the quantum dots play a critical role in determining their bandgap and emission wavelengths, rendering them highly versatile for various applications [36]. They demonstrate exceptional photoluminescence, making them vital in optoelectronics, with applications in LEDs, lasers, and photodetectors. Additionally, their small size allows for bioimaging at the cellular and molecular levels, aiding advances in medicine and biology. Semiconductor quantum dots hold promise in solar cells, enhancing light absorption and optimizing energy conversion efficiency. Furthermore, their utilization extends to biological and chemical sensing, where their unique properties offer heightened sensitivity and specificity. As a result, these nanocrystals continue to be a topic of extensive research, with their potential applications spanning a wide array of fields, from renewable energy to healthcare and beyond [37,38].

Metallic quantum dots represent a fascinating class of nanomaterials composed of metals or metal alloys at the nanoscale. Typically, smaller than 10 nm, these quantum dots possess unique optical, electronic, magnetic, and catalytic properties owing to their metallic nature and confined size [39]. They are characterized by localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), a collective oscillation of free electrons within the metal structure, leading to distinct optical features such as strong absorption and scattering of light. The LSPR phenomenon allows for tunable optical properties by altering the size, shape, and composition of the metallic quantum dots [40]. The morphology of these dots, whether spherical, rod-shaped, branched, or other configurations, influences their plasmonic properties and applications. Spherical metallic quantum dots are a common morphology, but anisotropic shapes like nanorods exhibit anisotropic plasmon resonances, enhancing their performance in applications like sensing and imaging. Applications of metallic quantum dots span a wide range, including bioimaging, biosensing, catalysis, plasmonic devices, and photodetectors [41]. They hold promise for use in therapeutic delivery, sensing of biological molecules, and improving the efficiency of various optoelectronic devices. The versatile and unique properties of metallic quantum dots position them at the forefront of nanotechnology, with ongoing research focused on enhancing their properties and exploring novel applications [42].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are nanoscale carbon-based particles, typically less than 10 nm in size, holding immense promise in the realm of nanotechnology. Composed primarily of carbon atoms arranged in a graphene-like or amorphous carbon structure, CQDs possess unique and tunable properties, making them highly versatile across various applications [43]. Their structure often involves a single layer of graphene or multiple layers with surface functional groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, crucial for their chemical and physical properties. Synthesized through diverse methods, including top-down approaches from larger carbon-rich materials or bottom-up approaches from small molecular precursors, CQDs exhibit strong photoluminescence with a wide absorption spectrum, allowing emission tuning from ultraviolet to visible light [44]. Quantum confinement effects due to their small size influence their electronic properties, resulting in quantized energy levels and a tunable bandgap. Surface functionalization plays a pivotal role in modifying and optimizing their properties for specific applications, including biomedical uses like bioimaging, drug delivery, and cancer therapy, owing to their biocompatibility and low toxicity. Additionally, CQDs find applications in sensors, energy conversion technologies, and environmental remediation, underlining their potential to revolutionize a multitude of fields [45].

Nanoparticles have many useful properties. In recent years, the use of a series of nanoparticles such as metal organic frameworks, nanofibers, nanoclusters, core–shell structures, and quantum dots has increased [46,47]. CQDs have unique and fascinating properties that have gained considerable attention in various scientific and technological fields. These dots, typically less than 10 nm in size, consist of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a crystalline or amorphous structure. One of their notable properties is their size-dependent optical behavior. CQDs exhibit exceptional photoluminescence, wherein their emission wavelength is inversely proportional to their size, making them versatile for applications in optoelectronics, bioimaging, and sensing. Moreover, CQDs possess a high surface area-to-volume ratio, enabling efficient surface functionalization and promoting interactions with biomolecules, making them valuable in bioapplications [44,48].

Their excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity further enhance their utility in biological and medical applications. CQDs exhibit remarkable electrical conductivity, allowing them to be used in electronic devices and as conductive additives in composite materials [49]. Additionally, their unique electronic properties make them promising candidates for energy-related applications, such as photovoltaics, photocatalysis, and energy storage. CQDs are also known for their outstanding chemical stability and environmental friendliness, adding to their appeal for sustainable technologies [50]. The versatile nature of carbon quantum dots, coupled with ongoing research and advancements, continues to unlock their potential across diverse domains, promising a bright future for their applications in cutting-edge technologies [51].
According to Fig. 1 , a more thorough categorization of CQDs is based on structural characteristics that take into account the chemical composition of the core and shell regions.

Xia et al. divided CQDs into four distinct classifications, including (i) graphene quantum dots (GQDs), (ii) carbon quantum dots (CQDs), (iii) carbon nanodots (CNDs), and (iv) carbon polymeric dots (CPDs), taking into account the core and shell structure. The most constant collection of graphene oxide layers pressed collectively is represented by GQDs. Due to the oxygen atoms present, GQDs are water-soluble fluorescent nanostructures that preserve the graphene lattices within the interlayer defects. Based on the synthetic method, whereby CQDs are formed by the carbonization of substrates and CNDs are generated through other processes, the difference between GQDs and CNDs is less distinct and questionable. It is clear that this categorization is flawed since materials classified as both GQDs and CNDs may overlap with other CDs and because it is not based on specific chemical characteristics. In reality, it is more beneficial to introduce carbon nitride quantum dots (CQNDs) as opposed to the GQD and CND formulations since it is more transparent to do so. CQNDs are comparable to GQDs but differ in that they have core structures that resemble multilayer C3N3 composites because they have nitrogen-doped graphene units rather than oxygen-based ones. Even though the number of studies in the field is small in comparison to CD research, the similarities between GQDs and CQNDs might point to a potential larger grouping that takes into account all hetero atom-doped GQDs (xGQDs, where x is N, O, B, S, Si, etc.). The CPD group, which includes species with distinct structures but no layered structure like graphene, is the most diverse group of CDs. CPDs can be created by the condensation of organic substances or by the thermal and chemical decomposition of synthetic and natural polymers. As a result, the existence and kind of heteroatoms or the precursors (polymers or minuscule molecules of organic material) might be used to sub-regroup CPDs into several groups. CPDs, which continue to be the most contentious group of CDs, cannot be precisely organized using an usual profile [52].

The synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a crucial process in nanomaterial science. CQDs are typically prepared through colloidal chemistry methods, involving the controlled growth of semiconductor nanocrystals in a solution. These nanocrystals exhibit unique optical and electronic properties due to quantum confinement effects. Characterization techniques, such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and UV–visible spectroscopy, are commonly used to assess the size, shape, crystal structure, and optical properties of CQDs. The precise control of CQD synthesis and thorough characterization are essential for tailoring their properties for various applications, including in optoelectronics, solar cells, and biological imaging.

CQD synthesis has been accomplished using a variety of demonstrated techniques. The purpose of a lot of studiesis to identify the most effective, environmentally friendly, and high-yielding approach for each synthetic chemosynthesis. Two basic methods—the top-down technique and the bottom-up approach—are used to produce CQDs. By using techniques including chemical oxidation, discharge, electrochemical oxidation, and ultrasonic procedures, bigger carbonaceous substances and structures can be broken down or cleaved. Nevertheless, this method has limitations, such as the need for expensive components, difficult reaction conditions, and prolonged reaction times. The bottom-up strategy, on the other hand, involves the transformation of smaller carbon structures into CQDs of the necessary size. Typical bottom-up methods are hydrothermal, microwave-Assisted, ultrasonication, biomass-derived precursors, microorganism-mediated methods which synthesize CQDs. Fig. 2 emphasizes the chemical distinction between the two methods, demonstrating how the bottom-up method creates the material from molecules while the top-down method breaks down the bulk material to produce CQDs [10].

Top-down approaches for the synthesis of CQDs involve the fabrication of CQDs from larger carbon structures, breaking them down into smaller nanoscale particles. One Top-down approach for the synthesis of CQDs is laser ablation, where a high-energy laser is employed to ablate a carbon source, typically graphite, leading to the formation of CQDs. The resulting particles are collected and can be further processed to control their size and properties. Another top-down method is electrochemical exfoliation, where a bulk carbon material, like graphite, is subjected to an electrolyte solution with an applied voltage. This process exfoliates the material into CQDs, which can be collected and modified for specific applications. These top-down approaches offer a degree of control over the size and properties of the CQDs, making them valuable in various fields, including optoelectronics, catalysis, and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal method for green synthesis of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal method for the green synthesis of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) represents an environmentally conscious approach to create these nanoscale carbon-based materials. Effective and safe strategies for the environment include protecting the environment and preventing environmental pollution [53]. This method capitalizes on the unique properties of water under elevated temperatures and pressures to convert carbon-rich precursors into CQDs, setting it apart from more conventional and environmentally taxing synthetic techniques [54]. Typically, the precursors employed in this process are sourced from natural, non-toxic materials such as fruit peels, tea leaves, or even glucose. These precursors, containing carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen atoms, are mixed with distilled water or another suitable solvent and placed in a sealed, high-pressure vessel designed to withstand the rigorous conditions of the hydrothermal process. The reaction vessel is then heated to temperatures typically below 200 °C, resulting in increased pressure due to the sealed environment. This setup allows for the synthesis to occur over a set period, often a few hours [55].

Once the hydrothermal reaction concludes, the vessel is carefully cooled down to room temperature. The resulting solution, now containing the newly formed CQDs, is isolated through methods such as filtration or centrifugation to remove any unreacted precursors or impurities. The synthesized CQDs can then undergo thorough characterization to analyze their size, morphology, and optical properties. The hydrothermal method offers several significant advantages, the foremost being its eco-friendliness. Using water as a solvent and operating at relatively low temperatures minimizes the environmental impact associated with high-temperature synthetic routes. Moreover, the hydrothermal method provides the ability to tune CQD properties, including size and surface functionalization, by adjusting various reaction parameters. Additionally, many hydrothermal syntheses rely on precursors derived from natural sources, further reducing the environmental footprint and waste generation. The method is also easily scalable, allowing for the production of larger quantities of CQDs when necessary. Altogether, the hydrothermal method stands as a versatile, sustainable, and green approach to CQD synthesis, making it highly promising for applications in fields like nanotechnology, sensing, and biomedicine [56].

Nguyen and their research team have introduced an innovative and sustainable approach for producing highly luminescent carbon dots and activated carbon through a cost-effective, environmentally friendly, and uncomplicated one-step hydrothermal method, employing banana peels as the primary source material. The resulting carbon dots exhibited robust and vibrant blue photoluminescence, boasting an average diameter in the range of 3 to 6 nm, rendering them particularly promising for a wide spectrum of applications, including biosensing, electronics, and catalysis research. What's more, the hydrothermal process led to the formation of activated carbon, characterized by its highly porous structure and an expansive specific surface area (294.6 m2/g). Consequently, the exceptional properties of these carbon dots underscore the significant potential of banana peels, as a readily available bioresource, in yielding two valuable and multifunctional products [57].

Wang et al harnessed the cost-effective potential of cyanobacteria biomass as the exclusive carbon source to create water-soluble Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) through a straightforward hydrothermal approach. The resultant CQDs displayed remarkable attributes, being uniformly mono-dispersed with an average diameter of 2.48 nm. The synthesis method's trifecta of virtues, namely, its green and sustainable approach, cost-effectiveness, and low cytotoxicity, collectively position the synthesized CQDs as a valuable carbon source. Furthermore, this approach serves to expand the horizons of cyanobacteria applications and offers an economically viable pathway for the large-scale production of CQDs. This research underscores the intersection of environmental responsibility, affordability, and innovation, creating new avenues for the practical use of CQDs and harnessing the potential of cyanobacteria resources. Fig. 3
a provides a schematic representation of the synthesis pathway for the CQDs derived from cyanobacteria. Wang and their collaborators proposed a plausible synthesis mechanism for these CQDs, as depicted in Fig. 3 b, drawing from insights gleaned from the chemical composition of cyanobacteria and pertinent analyses. Cyanobacteria, typically single-celled organisms, were found to predominantly consist of proteins and peptidoglycan. Elemental analysis revealed their elemental composition, primarily comprising carbon (37.87 %), hydrogen (8.04 %), nitrogen (7.62 %), and sulfur (0.97 %). It is rational to infer that, under thermal conditions, the cyanobacteria initially underwent hydrolysis, yielding substantial quantities of amino acids, N-acetylglucosamine acid, and N-acetylmuramic acid. Subsequently, polymerization processes took place during the synthetic procedure. The resulting soluble polymers then underwent carbonization, fostering the development and expansion of carbon cores within the CQDs. Importantly, owing to the involvement of intricate compounds in the synthetic reaction, the surfaces of these CQDs were inclined to host multiple functional groups following the reaction's conclusion. The utility of these CQDs, derived from cyanobacteria, extended to their application as fluorescent probes for biological imaging, as demonstrated in Fig. 3 c. This multifaceted approach not only elucidates the synthesis route but also sheds light on the diverse applications and potential of cyanobacteria-derived CQDs in the realm of bioimaging [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave-assisted method for green synthesis of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Microwave-Assisted method for the green synthesis of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) is a highly efficient and environmentally friendly approach to produce these nanomaterials. Unlike traditional synthesis methods, which often involve the use of hazardous chemicals and energy-intensive processes, the microwave-assisted method stands out for its sustainability and rapidity. In this approach, CQDs are synthesized from organic precursors such as carbohydrates, citric acid, or biomass-derived materials [59]. These precursors are chosen for their renewable and eco-friendly nature. The microwave irradiation provides the necessary energy for the synthesis, resulting in a highly controlled and rapid reaction. The microwave heating allows for the precise tuning of reaction parameters, such as temperature and time, which is crucial in tailoring the properties of CQDs, such as their size, morphology, and optical characteristics [60].

One of the key advantages of this green synthesis method is its energy efficiency. Microwave-assisted synthesis significantly reduces the energy consumption compared to traditional methods, which typically involve extended reaction times and higher temperatures. Moreover, it minimizes the generation of hazardous byproducts and waste, aligning with the principles of green chemistry [61]. Additionally, the controlled synthesis and efficient energy transfer within the microwave cavity lead to the production of CQDs with excellent uniformity and purity. The resulting CQDs synthesized using the microwave-assisted method exhibit exceptional optical properties, including strong fluorescence and tunable emission wavelengths, making them highly attractive for applications in sensors, imaging, and drug delivery systems [62]. Furthermore, this green synthesis approach is not only faster and more sustainable but also more scalable, offering great potential for large-scale CQD production with minimal environmental impact. Overall, the microwave-assisted method for the green synthesis of CQDs is a promising avenue for researchers seeking to harness the potential of these nanomaterials while minimizing their ecological footprint [63].

Sharma et al in their pioneering work, have developed a remarkable and ecologically conscious method for the production of biocompatible fluorescent CQDs. These CQDs, originating from the leaf extract of the renowned medicinal plant Calotropis gigantea, commonly referred to as the crown flower, are characterized by their sustainable, cost-effective, and user-friendly synthesis process. The resultant CQDs exhibit an intriguing size distribution, ranging from 2.7 to 10.4 nm, showcasing the versatility of this green synthesis approach. Advanced analytical techniques, including nuclear magnetic resonance analysis, have been employed to shed light on the CQDs' structural composition. The analysis revealed a substantial presence of sp2 and sp3 carbon groups within the CQDs. These findings are pivotal as they underscore the carbon-rich nature of the CQDs [64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasonication method for green synthesis of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The ultrasonication method for the green synthesis of CQDs represents an innovative and environmentally friendly approach to fabricate these nanomaterials. The ultrasonication method involves the use of high-frequency sound waves to generate localized cavitation and mechanical forces in a solution containing precursor materials. One of the notable advantages of this technique is its eco-friendliness. The synthesis process typically employs biocompatible and renewable precursors, such as plant extracts or biomass-derived compounds. By utilizing such sustainable starting materials, the ultrasonication method aligns with the principles of green chemistry by reducing the environmental impact associated with conventional chemical syntheses. The ultrasonication method's energy-efficient nature is another noteworthy aspect. It operates at lower temperatures and shorter reaction times compared to traditional methods, making it a greener alternative. Additionally, the controlled and uniform application of ultrasound waves enables precise tuning of reaction conditions, resulting in well-defined CQDs with tailored properties, including size, morphology, and surface functionalization [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biomass-derived precursors method for green synthesis of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The use of biomass-derived precursors in the green synthesis of CQDs represents an eco-friendly and sustainable approach for producing these nanomaterials. Biomass-derived precursors, which can include agricultural waste, plant extracts, or lignocellulosic materials, offer an abundant and renewable source for CQD synthesis, aligning with the principles of green chemistry and sustainability. The process typically involves subjecting the biomass-derived precursor to controlled chemical or thermal treatments [66]. These treatments break down the precursor into carbon-rich components that can be further processed to yield CQDs. The key advantage of this approach is the use of natural and biodegradable materials, reducing the reliance on non-renewable resources and mitigating the environmental impact associated with conventional synthesis methods. Biomass-derived CQDs are often characterized by their relatively low toxicity and biocompatibility, making them suitable for various applications in biomedicine, including drug delivery, imaging, and theranostics. Furthermore, the unique chemical composition of biomass-derived precursors can lead to CQDs with tailored properties, such as size, morphology, and surface functionality, offering versatility in their use [67].

Ding et al have made a significant contribution to sustainable nanomaterial synthesis by reporting an environmentally friendly method for producing Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) in an aqueous medium. This innovative approach leverages various commonly available crop biomasses as carbon sources, aligning with the principles of green chemistry and resource efficiency. The resulting CQDs not only exhibit robust and vibrant fluorescence but also possess excellent dispersion characteristics in commonly used solvents, accompanied by uniform particle size distribution. A distinguishing feature of these CQDs is their exceptional water dispersibility, which can be attributed to the presence of surface functional groups, including hydroxyl and carboxyl moieties. This property enhances their compatibility with aqueous systems and makes them well-suited for a range of applications in biomedicine, sensing, and environmental remediation. Beyond the scientific and technological significance, this research holds practical importance by showcasing the potential for transforming agricultural and crop waste materials into high-value products. By effectively converting crop biomass into CQDs with desirable properties, this work not only reduces the environmental burden associated with agricultural residues but also exemplifies a sustainable and circular approach to resource utilization. The eco-friendly synthesis of CQDs in water, using crop biomasses as carbon sources, sets the stage for advancements in both material science and waste-to-resource strategies, contributing to a more environmentally responsible and resource-efficient future [68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microorganism-mediated method for green synthesis of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The microorganism-mediated method for the green synthesis of CQDs is an innovative and sustainable approach that harnesses the power of microorganisms, such as bacteria or fungi, to produce these nanomaterials. This method stands out for its environmental friendliness and offers a unique avenue for CQD synthesis. Microorganisms are employed as biocatalysts in this process, using their inherent metabolic pathways to convert organic precursors into CQDs, thereby reducing the reliance on harsh chemicals and energy-intensive procedures commonly associated with traditional methods [69]. One of the key advantages of this method is its eco-friendly nature. Microorganisms typically thrive on renewable carbon sources, such as agricultural waste or biodegradable substrates, making this approach highly sustainable and in line with the principles of green chemistry. Moreover, the synthesis process can often occur under mild reaction conditions, minimizing energy consumption and reducing the generation of toxic byproducts, thus contributing to a cleaner and greener manufacturing process. The CQDs produced through microorganism-mediated synthesis exhibit unique and tunable properties. The size, morphology, and surface functionalization of these CQDs can be controlled by adjusting the growth conditions of the microorganisms. The resultant CQDs often possess excellent water dispersibility and biocompatibility, making them ideal for a wide range of applications [70].

Furthermore, the scalability of this method holds promise for large-scale CQD production. With the use of bioreactors and continuous cultivation strategies, it becomes feasible to produce CQDs in significant quantities while adhering to green synthesis principles. The microorganism-mediated method represents a pioneering approach to CQD synthesis that not only reduces the environmental footprint but also offers a sustainable pathway for the production of these versatile nanomaterials. As a result, it holds great potential for advancing various technological applications while prioritizing environmental sustainability.

Various synthesis methods for CQDs offer distinct advantages and are tailored to specific applications. The hydrothermal method is characterized by its simplicity, scalability, and versatility. It relies on high-temperature water-based reactions, allowing for controlled size and surface functionalization, making it suitable for bioimaging, sensing, drug delivery, and photocatalysis applications. Microwave-Assisted synthesis, on the other hand, stands out for its rapidity and energy efficiency. By utilizing microwave energy to drive reactions, this method offers precise control over the synthesis process, making it suitable for optoelectronics, sensors, drug delivery, and nanocomposite production. Ultrasonication, as a synthesis method, capitalizes on high-frequency sound waves, rendering it eco-friendly and energy-efficient. It provides fine control over CQD properties, including excellent water dispersibility, which makes it well-suited for applications in biomedicine, sensing, photocatalysis, and environmental remediation. In contrast, the biomass-derived precursor approach offers an eco-friendly and cost-effective alternative. It utilizes sustainable carbon sources from agricultural waste or plant extracts, delivering tunable CQDs with low toxicity. This method finds applications in various fields, including biomedicine, sensors, environmental remediation, and sustainable material production. The microorganism-mediated method stands out for its green and sustainable nature. It employs microorganisms as biocatalysts and operates under mild reaction conditions, offering tunable CQD properties. This method finds applications in biomedical imaging, drug delivery, and biotechnology. Each of these synthesis approaches showcases unique strengths, catering to a wide range of needs and preferences in CQD production, thereby advancing the development and applications of these versatile nanomaterials. A comparison of these synthesis methods can be seen in Table 1 .

Characterization of CQDs is a crucial process to gain insights into their properties and tailor their applications. There are various techniques for the characterization of CQDs, including Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–VIS spectroscopy photoluminescence (PL) and Electrostatic Force Microscopy (EFM).

Among various techniques, TEM is often considered the most important due to its versatility and depth of information. TEM allows for high-resolution imaging, revealing the size, morphology, and internal structure of CQDs, which is fundamental given their nanoscale dimensions. It also provides insights into the crystallinity and the presence of defects or surface structures, crucial for understanding their electronic and chemical properties. Moreover, TEM aids in assessing the homogeneity of CQD samples, ensuring consistency in applications such as sensors and bioimaging [76,77]. In a noteworthy study conducted by Almufarij and colleagues, the synthesis of CQDs was achieved through a hydrothermal process utilizing banana leaves as the precursor material. To understand the properties and characteristics of these CQDs, a comprehensive characterization process was undertaken, involving the utilization of TEM and XRD techniques, as illustrated in Fig. 4 . The TEM analysis, depicted in Fig. 4 a, offered intriguing insights into the morphology of the CQDs. The obtained TEM images revealed that the CQDs exhibited a distinct circular shape with a measured size of approximately 4.4 nm. This information is vital in assessing the nanoscale dimensions and structural attributes of the synthesized CQDs [78].

Concurrently, the XRD technique was employed to delve into the crystallographic nature of these CQDs. XRD is an instrumental characterization technique used to examine the crystallographic properties of CQDs [79]. It allows researchers to determine the crystal structure, thus revealing whether CQDs possess a crystalline or amorphous nature. The degree of crystallinity can also be gauged, with sharper diffraction peaks indicating higher order and organization within the material [80]. XRD aids in identifying the lattice parameters, which include unit cell dimensions and interatomic distances, offering insights into the specific atomic arrangement within CQDs. Moreover, it can help in distinguishing various phases and carbon structures in CQD samples, allowing for a comprehensive understanding of the diversity of CQDs present. XRD can also estimate the average size of crystalline domains within CQDs by analyzing peak broadening [81]. As captured in Fig. 4 b, the XRD micrograph displayed a prominent broad peak at a two-theta angle of 22.1°. This distinctive peak pattern suggested that the CQDs possessed a crystalline structure, implying the presence of well-defined and organized atomic arrangements within the nanomaterial [78].

AFM is a valuable technique used for the characterization of CQDs, particularly in understanding their morphology and surface topography at the nanoscale. In AFM, a sharp tip attached to a cantilever is scanned over the surface of the CQDs while measuring the forces acting on the tip. The tip-sample interaction provides information about the height and shape of CQDs [82]. AFM allows researchers to visualize and quantify the size and shape of CQDs with exceptional resolution. It provides three-dimensional images of individual CQDs or aggregates, revealing details about their surface roughness and structural features [83]. Additionally, AFM is highly useful for assessing the uniformity and distribution of CQDs within a sample, which is crucial for applications in areas such as nanoelectronics, sensors, and biomedicine. It can also be employed to investigate the impact of various factors, including surface functionalization or environmental conditions, on the surface properties of CQDs. Generally, AFM is an indispensable tool for gaining insights into the nanoscale morphology and structural characteristics of CQDs, contributing to a deeper understanding of their properties and potential applications [84]. In a study conducted by Gao et al Nitrogen-doped Carbon Quantum Dots (N-CQDs) were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal approach using naturally occurring biomass waste. In this work, different reducing agents such as orange peel and watermelon peel were used, which led to the production of orange peel carbon quantum dots (ON-CQDs) and watermelon peel carbon quantum dots (WN-CQDs), respectively. The synthesized N-CQDs underwent characterization using AFM. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the AFM analysis included low-magnification images (Fig. 5 a and 5c) with dimensions of 5 and 20 µm, respectively, and high-magnification images (Fig. 5 b and 5d) with dimensions of 2 µm. These dimensions were selected based on the sample morphology. The AFM images revealed that the distribution of N-CQDs exhibited acceptable consistency in terms of both size and shape [85].

FTIR is a highly valuable characterization technique applied to CQDs to elucidate their chemical composition, functional group identity, and surface chemistry. FTIR is pivotal in identifying and quantifying the various functional groups decorating the surface of CQDs, encompassing hydroxyl (OH), carboxyl (COOH), carbonyl (C = O), amino (NH2), and more. By scrutinizing the FTIR spectra, researchers can gain insights into the chemical composition of CQDs, distinguishing sources of these nanomaterials, and any chemical modifications they might have undergone during synthesis or post-synthesis treatments [86,87]. Rasooll et al undertook the synthesis of a novel nano-structured catalyst incorporating phosphorus acid moieties. This was achieved through a reaction between CQDs and phosphorus acid in refluxing ethanol (EtOH). The resultant material, denoted as CQDs-N(CH2PO3H2)2, was developed with a view to its versatile practical applications. The structure and morphology of CQDs-N(CH2PO3H2)2 were comprehensively investigated utilizing various characterization techniques, including FT-IR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectra of both CQDs and CQDs-N(CH2PO3H2)2 are juxtaposed in Fig. 6 for comparative analysis. Notably, the broad peak observed within the 2600–3500 cm−1 range is attributed to the presence of hydroxyl (OH) groups associated with the PO3H2 functional moieties. Additionally, distinct absorption bands manifest at 1015 and 1050 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching of P-O bonds, while a further band at 1128 cm−1 is indicative of P = O functionalities. The discrepancies between the FT-IR spectra of CQDs and CQDs-N(CH2PO3H2)2 are vividly illustrated in this figure, underscoring the successful incorporation of phosphorus acid moieties into the CQD structure [88].

UV–Vis spectroscopy, a fundamental tool in the characterization of CQDs, plays a pivotal role in unveiling critical insights into their optical properties. By exposing CQDs to ultraviolet and visible light, this technique allows researchers to examine their absorbance and emission behaviors [89]. In UV–Vis spectra, the absorbance peak indicates the wavelengths of light that CQDs absorb, providing information about their electronic band structure and bandgap. This data is indispensable for understanding the energy levels at which electrons in CQDs transition [90]. The position and intensity of the emission peak are crucial in assessing CQDs' quantum yield and emission characteristics. These optical properties are foundational for applications ranging from bioimaging to sensors, as they determine the CQDs' performance in various light-based technologies [55,91]. In a groundbreaking study, Janus et al engineered a novel variety of CQDs augmented with a rhodamine b derivative, aimed at amplifying their fluorescence capabilities while preserving their biocompatibility. The optical properties of these customized CQDs were meticulously examined through UV–VIS spectroscopy, as portrayed in Fig. 7 . The UV–VIS spectra of unaltered CQDs are showcased in Fig. 7 a, revealing distinct features. Within the diluted CQD solutions, two pronounced absorption peaks are evident, one displaying a sharp profile and another with broader characteristics. The former, centered around 200 nm, emanates from π → π* transitions associated with C = C groups resulting from the sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms. In contrast, the peaks observed at 293 nm (Dot-2), 298 nm (Dot-3), 301 nm (Dot-1), and 301 nm (Dot-4) correspond to n → π* transitions attributed to C = O groups. Turning to Fig. 7 b, the UV–VIS spectra of the quantum dots paired with the rhodamine derivative present a marked divergence from the raw CQDs (Fig. 7 a). New peaks emerge around 560 nm, characteristic of rhodamines, serving as compelling evidence of the successful modification in alignment with the findings in the FTIR spectra. This innovative approach promises enhanced fluorescence performance in CQDs while maintaining their biocompatible attributes, offering exciting prospects for applications across diverse domains [92].

One important method used to fully characterize CQDs is PL, which provides important information about their optical characteristics. The basic idea of photoluminescence is that a substance absorbs photons and then emits light as a result [93]. In the case of CQDs, energy is absorbed by the quantum dots upon exposure to a certain wavelength of light, usually in the UV or visible range, which excites electrons from the valence band to the conduction band. Light is released when these excited electrons eventually return to their initial condition. Researchers may learn vital details about the size distribution, surface chemistry, and electrical structure of CQDs by closely examining the light that is emitted [94]. In particular, photoluminescence emission spectra are a valuable source of information that provide information on the bandgap and energy levels of CQDs. CQDs with varying sizes or surface functionalizations frequently show different emission peaks, which may be used to make assumptions about their distribution and composition [95].

Beyond simple optical characteristics, photoluminescence plays a crucial role in figuring out CQDs' quantum yield, a measure of the light emission efficiency. Greater quantum yields are a sign of more effective materials for photoluminescence. This method also helps to assess how surface passivation and functionalization affect the optical characteristics of CQDs [96]. Surface alterations can affect the wavelength and intensity of emission, allowing CQDs to be customized for particular uses. The size-dependent emission phenomena in CQD photoluminescence is one noteworthy feature [97]. For bigger and smaller CQDs, quantum confinement effects can produce different emission characteristics, which helps to explain their size-dependent optical behavior. Furthermore, CQD photoluminescence is useful for sensing applications because to its sensitivity to environmental variables like temperature and pH. The development of CQD-based sensors for various applications is made possible by changes in emission characteristics in response to environmental variables [98].

Saafie et al developed kenaf-based carbon quantum dots (CQD) were prepared. The as-prepared CQDs have been characterized in detail by PL technique. Fig. 8 illustrates the PL spectra of all the CQDs samples n the wavelength of 380–650 nm. Unlike typical PL spectra CQDs reported, kenaf-based CQDs displayed a multi-peak emission feature at ∼ 400 and ∼ 450 nm. The emission peak at ∼ 400 nm is attributed to the fluorescence of the carbon nucleus and the emission peak at ∼ 450 nm is attributed to the flourescence caused by the nano-interparticles distances due to the CQDs agglomeration. The narrow PL emission area with the emission band centred at ∼ 400 nm validates the narrow size distribution of CQDs [99].

EFM is an advanced atomic force microscopy (AFM) technique employed in the characterization of materials at the nanoscale, and it holds particular significance in the study of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [100]. Operating on the principles of AFM, EFM introduces an additional dimension by applying a bias voltage between the sharp tip of the cantilever and the sample surface. This bias voltage induces an electrostatic force that interacts with the local electrical properties of the sample, making EFM a powerful tool for investigating surface potential variations. In the context of carbon quantum dots, EFM plays a crucial role in elucidating their electrical characteristics. By utilizing the electrostatic force between the charged AFM tip and the CQD surface, EFM can provide detailed maps of the local surface potential distribution. This capability is particularly valuable for understanding the charge distribution on the CQD surface, offering insights into the impact of factors such as functionalization, defects, and heterogeneity [101].

One key application of EFM in the study of carbon quantum dots lies in unraveling their electrical heterogeneity. CQDs often exhibit variations in size, shape, and surface functionalization, leading to inherent differences in their electrical properties. EFM allows researchers to quantitatively analyze the surface potential, enabling a comprehensive understanding of the electrical heterogeneity across individual CQDs [102]. Furthermore, EFM can be instrumental in investigating the effects of surface functionalization on CQDs, providing a means to observe alterations in surface potential resulting from different functional groups. Additionally, EFM facilitates the examination of defects and charge traps on the surface of carbon quantum dots, which are essential considerations for comprehending their electronic properties [103]. The technique enables researchers to map out variations in surface potential associated with defects, offering valuable information about the charge-trapping mechanisms within CQDs. Overall, while EFM serves as a powerful tool for probing the electrical properties of carbon quantum dots, a holistic characterization often involves a synergistic combination of EFM with other analytical techniques, such as spectroscopy and microscopy, to provide a comprehensive understanding of the structural and electronic aspects of CQDs [104].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of antibacterial resistance of CQDs
Mechanisms of antibacterial activity of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Different nanomaterials such as core–shell, nano stars, nanofibers, metal–organic frameworks, covalent organic frameworks, quantum dots and others have many uses in various fields [105–107]. As mentioned CQDs are a class of nanomaterials that have gained significant attention in recent years due to their unique properties and potential applications in various fields, including biomedicine. One of the interesting properties of carbon quantum dots is their antibacterial activity. The antibacterial activity of CQDs is underpinned by several intricate mechanisms [108]. Firstly, CQDs have the ability to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) when exposed to light or other stimuli. These ROS, including singlet oxygen and superoxide radicals, are highly reactive and can inflict damage on bacterial cell components, notably the cell membrane and biomolecules. This damage can lead to cell membrane disruption, affecting its integrity and permeability, thereby resulting in the leakage of cellular contents and eventually cell death [109]. Moreover, CQDs, with their small size and unique surface properties, can interact with bacterial membranes directly, interfering with the membrane's structural integrity and function [110]. This interaction can also disrupt the electrochemical gradients across the membrane, causing an imbalance in ion transport and further compromising the bacterial cell's viability. In addition to their impact on cell membranes, CQDs have been found to interfere with biofilm formation, a protective mechanism employed by bacteria to evade immune responses and antibiotics [111]. CQDs can disrupt the biofilm structure, making bacteria more vulnerable to traditional antibiotics and other antibacterial agents. Collectively, these multifaceted mechanisms contribute to the antibacterial activity of CQDs, enabling them to combat a wide range of bacterial species, both Gram-positive and Gram-negative, and make them promising candidates for biomedical applications, such as wound dressings and photodynamic therapy [112,113]. However, ongoing research is essential to fine-tune these mechanisms, optimize CQD synthesis methods, and explore their long-term safety and efficacy in clinical settings.

The HSER (Homo sapiens retinoic acid receptor) peptide was created by Mazumdar et al. from the retinoic acid receptor responder protein 2 of Homo sapiens and was conjugated with synthetic CQDs (carbon quantum dots) for increased antibacterial properties since they are not highly efficient used alone. The antibacterial effectiveness against Escherichia coli (carbapenem-resistant) and Vancomycin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus (VRSA) was next examined using growth curve analysis, which was further corroborated by microscopic pictures indicating the presence of cell debris and dead bacterial cells. It was discovered that HSER-CQDs' antibacterial mechanism involves breaking down the cell membrane and interacting with genomic DNA (gDNA). The HSER-CQD interaction is based on a change in HSER structure following coating on the CQD surface, which results in cell wall interaction and cell wall disruption. Loss of cell integrity results in the leakage of intercellular components without the generation of ROS [114]. (Fig. 9 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influences parameter on antibacterial resistance of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The influences and parameters affecting the antibacterial resistance of CQDs are multifaceted and can determine the efficacy of CQDs as antibacterial agents [115,116]. Some key factors include:
•
CQD Size and Structure: The size and structure of CQDs can significantly influence their antibacterial activity. Smaller CQDs may have greater surface area and be more effective in penetrating bacterial cells or disrupting their membranes. The size and shape of CQDs can also affect their ROS generation capacity, which is a key mechanism of antibacterial action [117].
•
Surface Chemistry: The surface chemistry of CQDs plays a crucial role in their interactions with bacteria. Functional groups on the CQD surface can enhance or diminish their antibacterial activity. Some functional groups may facilitate binding to bacterial cells, while others may affect ROS generation and other mechanisms [118].
•
ROS Generation: The ability of CQDs to generate ROS is a significant contributor to their antibacterial activity. The type and amount of ROS generated can depend on factors such as CQD composition, exposure to light, and the presence of oxygen. Fine-tuning the ROS generation can be essential for optimizing antibacterial efficacy [119].
•
Light Activation: CQDs can exhibit enhanced antibacterial activity when exposed to specific wavelengths of light. The choice of light source, duration of exposure, and light intensity can influence the degree of bacterial cell damage. Light activation allows for precise control over the antibacterial effects of CQDs [120].
•
Bacterial Species and Strains: The susceptibility of different bacterial species and strains to CQDs can vary. Some bacteria may have protective mechanisms or adaptations that reduce their vulnerability to CQD-induced damage. Understanding the specific strains being targeted is crucial for the effective application of CQDs [30].
•
CQD Concentration: The concentration of CQDs used in antibacterial applications can affect their efficacy. The optimal concentration may vary depending on the specific application and the bacterial strain. Higher concentrations may lead to more pronounced antibacterial effects, but it's essential to balance this with cytotoxicity concerns [121].
•
Environmental Conditions: Environmental factors, such as pH and temperature, can influence the interactions between CQDs and bacteria. CQD stability and ROS generation may be affected by these conditions, which can, in turn, impact antibacterial resistance [122].
•
Biofilm Formation: Bacterial biofilms are resistant structures that protect bacteria from external threats. CQDs may disrupt biofilm formation, making bacteria more vulnerable to antibacterial agents. The ability of CQDs to interfere with biofilms can be a critical parameter [123].
•
Duration of Exposure: The duration of CQD exposure to bacteria can influence the extent of antibacterial activity. Longer exposure times may lead to more significant bacterial damage, but this must be balanced with considerations of practicality and safety in various applications [124].

In summary, the antibacterial resistance of CQDs is influenced by various parameters, including CQD size, structure, surface chemistry, ROS generation, light activation, bacterial species, concentration, environmental conditions, biofilm interference, and exposure duration. Optimizing these parameters is crucial for the effective use of CQDs as antibacterial agents in different applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages and disadvantages of CQDs in antibacterial performance
ce:section.ce:para: The use of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) in antibacterial applications offers several advantages, making them promising candidates for combating bacterial infections. Some of the key advantages include: Broad-Spectrum Antibacterial Activity: CQDs have demonstrated antibacterial activity against a wide range of bacterial species, including both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. This broad-spectrum activity makes them versatile and applicable in various medical and non-medical contexts [125]. Low Cytotoxicity to Mammalian Cells: CQDs have been found to exhibit low cytotoxicity to mammalian cells at effective antibacterial concentrations. This is a crucial advantage, as it means that CQDs can selectively target and kill bacteria without harming human cells, making them safer for medical use. Light-Activated Antibacterial Properties: CQDs can be activated by exposure to specific wavelengths of light. This feature allows for precise control over their antibacterial effects. It enables selective targeting of bacteria, minimizing damage to surrounding healthy tissues in photodynamic therapy and other applications. Reduced Antibiotic Resistance: The multifaceted antibacterial mechanisms of CQDs, including ROS generation and disruption of bacterial membranes, can reduce the likelihood of bacteria developing resistance [126]. This can help overcome the growing problem of antibiotic resistance in pathogenic bacteria. Biofilm Disruption: CQDs have been shown to disrupt the formation of bacterial biofilms. Biofilms are protective structures that bacteria use to evade the immune system and antibiotics. CQDs can interfere with biofilm formation, making bacteria more vulnerable to traditional antibacterial treatments [127]. Potential for Combination Therapies: CQDs can be combined with other antibacterial agents, such as antibiotics or other nanomaterials, to enhance their overall antibacterial performance [128]. This synergistic effect can improve the treatment of bacterial infections. Customizable Properties: CQDs can be synthesized with various properties, such as size, surface chemistry, and functionalization, allowing for customization based on the specific requirements of the antibacterial application. Environmental Friendliness: CQDs are carbon-based nanomaterials, which are considered more environmentally friendly compared to some other nanomaterials [129]. They are less likely to cause ecological harm, especially when designed for biomedical use. Biomedical Applications: CQDs are being explored for various biomedical applications, including wound dressings, drug delivery systems, and photodynamic therapy for bacterial infections. Their low cytotoxicity and versatility make them suitable for integration into medical devices and therapies. Research and Innovation: The field of CQDs and their antibacterial properties is relatively new, which means ongoing research and innovation are likely to lead to further improvements and applications in the future [130].

While CQDs offer several advantages in antibacterial applications, they also come with certain disadvantages and challenges [131]. First lack of Long-term Safety Data: CQDs are a relatively new class of nanomaterials, and there is a limited amount of long-term safety data available regarding their use in antibacterial applications [132]. This lack of comprehensive data makes it difficult to assess potential risks and unknown effects that might arise over extended periods. Complex Synthesis Methods: The synthesis of CQDs can involve complex chemical processes, and the use of various chemicals and precursors. This complexity can be a challenge both in terms of production and potential environmental impacts associated with their manufacturing. Scalability: While CQDs show promise on a small scale, their scalability for large-scale antibacterial applications can be limited [133]. Optimizing the manufacturing process to produce CQDs in sufficient quantities for widespread use is an ongoing challenge. Safety and Allergic Reactions: While CQDs are generally considered safe for mammalian cells, there is always a potential for unknown side effects, allergic reactions, or hypersensitivity reactions in some individuals. Extensive safety testing is necessary before widespread clinical or environmental use [134]. Light Activation Challenges: In light-activated antibacterial applications, maintaining consistent and precise light conditions can be challenging, especially in clinical settings. It may be difficult to ensure that the required light exposure is always available [135]. Customization Complexity: While the customizable properties of CQDs are advantageous, they also add complexity to their development. Tailoring CQDs for specific antibacterial needs requires careful design, which can be time-consuming and challenging [136]. Potential for Bacterial Resistance: Although the potential for bacterial resistance to CQDs is lower compared to traditional antibiotics, it is still a concern. Continued monitoring and research are necessary to assess the long-term efficacy and the development of any resistance mechanisms [137].

In summary, while CQDs offer potential advantages in antibacterial applications, these disadvantages underscore the need for further research, safety assessments, and the refinement of their production and application methods to ensure their safe and effective use in various contexts.

Various sciences have made great progress in various fields [138,139], one of the most interesting of these sciences in recent years is antibacterial resistance. CQDs have emerged as a versatile class of nanomaterials with applications spanning a wide array of fields. Their unique properties, including exceptional biocompatibility, tunable emission properties, and low cytotoxicity, have paved the way for their use in various applications. CQDs find utility in fields such as bioimaging [140], drug delivery [141], energy conversion [142], and sensing [143], highlighting their capacity to revolutionize a multitude of scientific and technological domains. Particularly noteworthy is their role in addressing the global challenge of antibacterial resistance [144–146]. CQDs have demonstrated remarkable antibacterial activity against a diverse range of bacteria, encompassing both Gram-positive and Gram-negative strains. In the following, we will review the studies that have been done in this field in recent years.

Zhao et al successfully synthesized quaternized carbon quantum dots (qCQDs) with an impressive broad-spectrum antibacterial capability. Their innovative approach utilized a straightforward and environmentally friendly “one-pot” method, employing dimethyl diallyl ammonium chloride and glucose as reaction precursors. The resulting qCQDs exhibited remarkable antibacterial activity against a wide range of bacteria, effectively targeting both Gram-positive and Gram-negative strains. To evaluate their practical efficacy, qCQDs were tested in rat models with wounds infected by mixed bacterial strains. The results were highly promising: qCQDs not only restored the weight of the afflicted rats but also significantly reduced the mortality rate associated with severe infections. Furthermore, these nanomaterials played a pivotal role in accelerating the healing and recovery of infected wounds. Importantly, biosafety assessments indicated that qCQDs posed no discernible toxic or adverse effects during testing, underlining their potential for safe use in antibacterial applications. To uncover the underlying molecular mechanisms behind qCQDs' antibacterial prowess, quantitative proteomics analyses were conducted. These revealed that qCQDs primarily targeted ribosomal proteins in Staphylococcus aureus (a Gram-positive bacterium) and markedly down-regulated proteins associated with the citrate cycle in Escherichia coli (a Gram-negative bacterium). Furthermore, real-time quantitative PCR studies validated the consistency of gene expression trends corresponding to the proteins involved in ribosomal and citrate cycle functions post-treatment with qCQDs. These findings strongly suggest that qCQDs operate through a unique antibacterial mechanism distinct from those reported for other carbon quantum dots, shedding light on their potential for innovative antibacterial applications [147].

Nanofibrous constructs derived from nanoparticles and biopolymers have garnered significant attention in the field of tissue engineering due to their striking resemblance to the natural extracellular matrix. Norouzi and their team embarked on a research endeavor to synthesize electrospun nanofibrous mats composed of chitosan-carbon quantum dots-titanium dioxide-graphene oxide (CS-CQD-TiO2-GO) via the electrospinning technique, with the intention of employing them as advanced wound dressings. Remarkably, their findings from antimicrobial tests unveiled the exceptional antibacterial properties of these fabricated nanofibrous mats against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacterial strains. The viability of treated mouse fibroblast NIH3T3 cells in contact with the nanofibrous mat was assessed through an MTT assay, demonstrating appropriate cell compatibility. Furthermore, animal studies unequivocally affirmed the promising potential of these nanofibrous mats in the realm of wound dressing applications. Past research has consistently emphasized that Staphylococcus aureus bacteria serve as primary culprits in wound infections, while Escherichia coli is a notorious contributor to burn wound infections, both significantly impeding the wound healing process. The study conducted by Norouzi and their team focused on the Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) values of CQD-TiO2-GO-Cs nanocomposites, which were found to be 0.2 mg·mL−1 for Staphylococcus aureus and 0.1 mg·mL−1 for Escherichia coli. Notably, paper discs were impregnated with these nanocomposites at the aforementioned concentrations, and as controls, gentamicin and tetracycline discs were employed. Results illustrated in Fig. 10
A indicated an average halo diameter of approximately 12 mm around the nanocomposite disk for Staphylococcus aureus, while the halo diameter surrounding the tetracycline antibiotic disk averaged around 21 mm. As evident in Fig. 10 C, the inhibition zone for S. aureus was strikingly similar around both the nanocomposite and gentamicin antibiotic disks. Meanwhile, Fig. 10 B revealed a mean halo diameter of 27 mm around the nanocomposite disk for E. coli, while the average halo diameter encircling the tetracycline antibiotic disk reached approximately 29 mm. The inhibition zones created by the gentamicin antibiotic and the nanocomposite disk, showcased in Fig. 10 D, were also closely comparable. Notably, the antibacterial effectiveness was notably more pronounced against Gram-negative E. coli compared to Gram-positive S. aureus, likely attributed to variations in their cell wall structures [148].

In the face of a growing global threat posed by multidrug-resistant bacterial infections, the imperative to develop novel antibiotics has never been more pressing. Curcumin, a natural compound renowned for its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, presents a promising avenue. However, its limited water solubility has long hindered its bioavailability, curbing its clinical application and overall bioactivity. In response to this challenge, Wu et al tried to enhance curcumin's bioactivity and broaden its spectrum of antibacterial capabilities. The solution came in the form of quaternized carbon quantum dots (Q-CQDs) crafted from curcumin and 2,3-epoxypropyltrimethylammonium chloride (GTA) through a meticulous “double-thermal” process. This approach not only drastically improved the solubility and stability of curcumin but also retained its active properties and introduced quaternary ammonium groups to amplify its antibacterial potential. Experimental findings unveiled the remarkable broad-spectrum antibacterial prowess of Q-CQDs, far surpassing that of natural curcumin. A closer look into the antibacterial mechanisms at play revealed that Q-CQDs, adorned with -N+(CH3)3 groups, displayed robust adherence to bacterial cell membranes. Operating like a metaphorical “Trojan Horse,” these nanostructures compromised the integrity of bacterial cells upon entry. This breach unleashed a cascade of events, including the generation of ROS and the efflux of cytoplasmic DNA and RNA, ultimately culminating in bacterial demise. Crucially, the development of bacterial resistance to Q-CQDs was conspicuously absent, and these nanomaterials exhibited no hemolytic or cytotoxic effects. The potential of Q-CQDs in clinical applications was further substantiated through in vivo studies involving Staphylococcus aureus-infected wounds, E-coli-infected wounds, and wounds infected with a mix of bacterial strains in a mouse model. These investigations demonstrated that Q-CQDs not only curtailed bacterial populations at wound sites but also ameliorated inflammation and accelerated the wound healing process. These compelling results signify Q-CQDs as promising candidates for addressing infected wound healing in clinical settings, potentially extending their application to combat bacterial infections resistant to conventional treatments [149].

Yan and colleagues embarked on a pioneering endeavor, harnessing the power of nature's own turmeric to craft a novel antibacterial material known as ST-JHCQDs through a straightforward and environmentally friendly approach. This remarkable process leveraged turmeric's inherent properties as a carbon source and saw ST-JHCQDs meticulously characterized through a battery of physical and optical analyses. In a series of in vitro antibacterial investigations, it became evident that the efficacy of ST-JHCQDs against Escherichia coli (E. coli) and S. aureus was closely intertwined with variables such as carbonization degrees, concentration, and the duration of exposure to blue light. As the research delved deeper, biomolecule leakage and meticulous confocal laser scanning microscopy revealed an essential facet of ST-JHCQDs' antibacterial prowess. Under the influence of blue light irradiation, these nanomaterials demonstrated a remarkable capacity to generate ROS. The consequences were twofold: the disruption of cell membrane integrity and the leakage of intracellular macromolecules in both bacterial strains. Complementary to this, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images painted a vivid picture of cell membrane wrinkling and fragmentation. These structural alterations mirrored the detrimental effects of ROS, thus serving as tangible evidence of ST-JHCQDs' effectiveness as photosensitizers in the realm of in vitro photodynamic antimicrobial activity. This body of experiments underscores the immense potential held by carbon quantum dots derived from turmeric as a novel, natural photosensitizer material. With its capacity to induce antibacterial effects through ROS generation under blue light, ST-JHCQDs open new horizons in the quest for innovative and sustainable antibacterial solutions [150].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Future and perspective of green synthesis of CQDs in antibacterial resistance
ce:section.ce:para: The future of green synthesis of CQDs holds great promise, driven by the increasing global awareness of environmental sustainability and the growing demand for innovative nanomaterials. Green synthesis methods, which emphasize eco-friendliness and low environmental impact, are expected to play a pivotal role in this evolution. CQDs, with their unique properties and diverse applications in fields like nanotechnology, biomedicine, and optoelectronics, are poised to benefit from such environmentally friendly approaches. In the coming years, the perspective of green CQD synthesis will likely witness significant advancements in several key areas. Firstly, researchers are expected to develop novel, energy-efficient, and scalable synthesis methods that utilize sustainable precursors, such as biomass-derived materials, as carbon sources. These methods will reduce the carbon footprint associated with CQD production and make them more economically viable. Additionally, the exploration of green solvents and the reduction of hazardous chemicals in CQD synthesis will further enhance their environmental profile. Furthermore, the tailoring of CQDs with specific properties, such as size, surface functionalization, and optical characteristics, will be a focal point. This tailoring will enable the fine-tuning of CQDs for specialized applications, ranging from drug delivery and bioimaging to energy storage and environmental monitoring. Collaboration between academia, industry, and regulatory bodies will be vital to ensure that green CQD synthesis aligns with sustainable development goals and stringent environmental standards. As the demand for sustainable materials continues to grow, CQDs synthesized through environmentally friendly means will likely become a key player in addressing both scientific and environmental challenges. The future and perspective of green CQD synthesis are promising, as it aligns with the global shift toward more sustainable and responsible materials production.

The future and perspective of green synthesis of CQDs hold significant potential in addressing the global challenge of antibacterial resistance. Antibiotic-resistant bacteria are a growing threat to public health, necessitating innovative solutions.
